desc: Large silkmoth of mountain pine forests. Periodic outbreaks defoliate pines. Larvae edible — Native American food source.
Colorful day-flying silkmoth. Pink, yellow, and black. Larvae on wild rose, ceanothus.
Day-flying silkmoth of chaparral. Black with white band. Larvae gregarious with stinging spines.
Yellow moth with prominent hindwing eyespots. Larvae have urticating spines. Rare in SoCal.
Brown sphinx moth with blue and pink hindwing eyespots. Larvae on cherry and plum.
Large gray sphinx moth on cottonwoods and willows along riparian corridors.
Olive and pink sphinx moth. Larvae on Galium (bedstraw) and Epilobium (fireweed).
Day-flying moth that mimics a bumble bee. Hovers at flowers. Clear wings with dark margins.
Communal tent-building caterpillars on oaks and other hardwoods. Periodic outbreaks.
White moth. Caterpillars spin communal webs on branch tips in fall. Introduced from NA to worldwide.
Brown noctuid. Major agricultural pest — larvae feed on many crops.
Small moth with translucent wings. Larvae defoliate coast live oaks. Endemic to California.
Small metallic blue-black day-flying moth. Gregarious larvae skeletonize grape leaves.
Small tortricid moth. Pest of citrus and ornamentals. Widespread in urban areas.
Males brown, winged; females wingless. Caterpillars with tufts of hair. On oaks and many trees.
Female wingless. Larvae (inchworms) on oaks. Related to eastern spring cankerworm.
Silver-marked noctuid. Larvae are loopers on many crops. Important baculovirus host for biocontrol research.
Pale geometrid moth with dark wing borders. Nocturnal. Attracted to lights.
Small moth — larva is the classic 'worm in the apple.' Introduced worldwide from Central Asia.
Major pest of almonds, walnuts, and citrus. Can carry aflatoxin-producing fungi.
Expanding its range westward. Larvae cut bean and corn seedlings at soil level.
Cosmopolitan stored-product pest. Bronze and cream pattern. Found in homes and warehouses.
Large green sphinx moth with pink patches. Larvae on grape and Virginia creeper. At range limit.
Day-flying bumblebee mimic with transparent wings. Hovers at penstemons, salvias.
Brown prominent moth. Larvae on willows and poplars with bizarre forked tail process.
